data.title: 'Running A Small Business Is Exhausting,' Says A Coffee Shop Owner. 'I Used To Like Coffee. Now It Just Haunts My Dreams'
data.description:
Running a small coffee shop was supposed to be about community, good conversations and, of course, great coffee. Instead, one owner who has been running a small shop for a couple of years says it has turned into a nonstop grind of stress, repairs and paperwork.

“I used to like coffee,” the owner wrote in a recent Reddit post. “Now it just haunts my dreams.”

The Messy Middle Of Small Business

The breaking point that day was an espresso machine that “decided to give up on life” right in the middle of the morning rush. Customers were waiting. Orders were piling up. Repair calls had to be made. And the complaints started rolling in.

Don't Miss:

  • The ‘ChatGPT of Marketing' Just Opened a $0.85/Share Round — 10,000+ Investors Are Already In
  • It’s no wonder Jeff Bezos holds over $250 million in art — this alternative asset has outpaced the S&P 500 since 1995, delivering an average annual return of 11.4%. Here’s how everyday investors are getting started.

“Running a small business is exhausting,” the owner said. “Spent the whole day calling in repair guys and dealing with cranky customers who just want their caffeine fix.”

But the espresso machine was just the latest problem in what the owner described as a “constant juggling act” that never seems to end. Staffing. Inventory. Marketing. Compliance. And then, always, paperwork.

“Why is there always so much paperwork?” the owner asked. “Do they really need to know every single bean I buy?”

That frustration struck a chord with other small business owners. “We don’t own a small business, it owns us,” one replied.  Another said people constantly tell them how lucky they are not to have a boss. “I LOL every time,” they wrote.

Several owners pointed out that customers, employees, vendors and regulators all become bosses in their own way. “We are here to not have a boss, which actually turns out we have about 1,000 small bosses,” one commenter joked.

Trending: Disney Was Built on Character IP — This Pre-IPO Company Is Using the Same Playbook

Many described years two and three as the hardest stretch. Big enough to be complicated, but not organized enough to feel stable. “Year 2 was utter hell,” one bakery owner wrote. “Year 3 was just regular hell.”

Equipment failures were a common theme. “Everything and I mean everything starts to break in the 3-5 year range,” said an alcohol business owner with $1.3 million in annual sales. Once warranties expire, the capital expenses “kick in the teeth,” they added.

Death By A Thousand Small Problems

Others emphasized that the real exhaustion doesn’t come from one catastrophic event. It comes from constant small interruptions. A staff member calls in sick. A supplier runs out of stock. A permit needs renewing. Freight costs rise. Something leaks. Something breaks.

“Small businesses aren’t exhausting because of one big problem,” one commenter wrote. “They’re exhausting because of constant small interruptions.”

The mental load, many agreed, never switches off. Even when the doors close, the responsibility remains.

Still, not everyone would trade it for a steady office job. “If I knew it was gonna be like this I wouldn’t have started,” one service business owner admitted. “However, I cannot imagine going back to an office job.”

Several owners said the turning point came when they stopped trying to do everything themselves. “Systematize everything so if something happens your staff know what to do,” one wrote. “Delegate trust,”  Another advised creating simple checklists for “what to do when X breaks.”

Check out their venture portfolio today. Start investing in minutes and with as little as $10.

As for the coffee shop owner, the question remains whether the stress is temporary growing pains or a signal to make changes. “Sometimes I wonder if I should just pack it up and find a nice quiet office job,” they wrote.

For now, at least, they are still brewing.

Image: Shutterstock
